coredata.dc:title: Understanding the graphene quantum dots-ubiquitin interaction by identifying the interaction sites
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Ge
Luan, Binquan
Ge, Cuicui
Chong, Yu
Dong, Xu
Guo, Jun
Tang, Chun
Zhou, Ruhong
coredata.dc:description: A thorough understanding of the molecular mechanisms of protein-nanoparticle associations facilitates the development of novel applications in nanoscience and nanotechnology. As an important functional protein that mediates protein degradation pathway, ubiquitin is abundant and regulates a large number of cellular processes. Thus, in this study, we investigated, with a combined experimental and theoretical approach, the interaction of ubiquitin with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an important class of novel carbon nanomaterials which has attracted intense attention lately due to its great potential in biomedical applications. Our surface plasmon resonance (SPR) studies indicated a high binding affinity between ubiquitin and GQDs, and the circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy revealed a marked reduction in ubiquitin's β-sheet content. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy unambiguously identified the ubiquitin-GQDs interaction sites at the residue level, with majority of them residing near the β-sheet regions. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations further revealed the microscopic picture of the ubiquitin-GQDs interaction at atomic detail, supporting the experimental findings. Overall, we provide a deep comprehension of protein-GQDs interaction through identification and characterization of the key binding sites, which may pave the way for a better guidance on safe design and development of potential applications using graphene nanomaterials.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is partially supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program Grant No. 2014CB931900), National Natural Science Foundation of China (11575123, 11574224 and 11374221), Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Radiation Medicine and Protection, a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of GQDs. a) TEM image（Top-right inset shows the HRTEM image） b) AFM images c) The UV-visible spectrum d) The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

GQDs@ubiquitin. a) Measurements of GQDs-ubiquitin binding affinity. Association and dissociation curves of ubiquitin interactions with GQDs were presented. Affinity for the GQDs-ubiquitin interactions was determined from the curve. b) The circular dichroism spectra of free ubiquitin and GQDs@ubiquitin with the mass ratio of 0:1, 1:1, 1:7 and 4:1 (GQDs:ubiquitin). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Superposition of 1H 15N HSQC spectra of samples of free ubiquitin (cyan-blue) and GQDs@ubiquitin corona (red). b) NMR chemical shift perturbation analysis of ubiquitin upon interaction with GQDs. CSP is given by the weighted average chemical shift differences Δδ. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Simulated contacts between ubiquitin and graphene. (a–j) contact probabilities of protein residues with graphene in ten independent MD simulations labeled reversely from 10 to 1 in (k), respectively. Green lines signify the residues that were found with non-negligible CSP in experiment. (k) Interaction energy for each simulated contact between graphene and ubiquitin. The blue line is for the first-kind (Type 1) contact and the orange line is for the second kind contact (Type 2). (l, m, n) Snap-shots of detailed ubiquitin-graphene contacts, that correspond to results shown in (a, b, c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Understanding the graphene quantum dots-ubiquitin interaction by identifying the interaction sites

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: IBM Thomas J. Watson Research Center

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A thorough understanding of the molecular mechanisms of protein-nanoparticle associations facilitates the development of novel applications in nanoscience and nanotechnology. As an important functional protein that mediates protein degradation pathway, ubiquitin is abundant and regulates a large number of cellular processes. Thus, in this study, we investigated, with a combined experimental and theoretical approach, the interaction of ubiquitin with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), an important class of novel carbon nanomaterials which has attracted intense attention lately due to its great potential in biomedical applications. Our surface plasmon resonance (SPR) studies indicated a high binding affinity between ubiquitin and GQDs, and the circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy revealed a marked reduction in ubiquitin's β-sheet content. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy unambiguously identified the ubiquitin-GQDs interaction sites at the residue level, with majority of them residing near the β-sheet regions. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations further revealed the microscopic picture of the ubiquitin-GQDs interaction at atomic detail, supporting the experimental findings. Overall, we provide a deep comprehension of protein-GQDs interaction through identification and characterization of the key binding sites, which may pave the way for a better guidance on safe design and development of potential applications using graphene nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have been increasingly used in biomedicine and biotechnology, such as drug delivery, disease diagnosis and therapy [1,2]. It is now widely accepted that, when nanomaterials enter circulation and cells, they will inevitably interact with biological macromolecules (most of which are proteins) leading to formation of a protein corona that largely redefines the biological identity of the nanomaterials, and thus result in unpredictable biological reactions (e.g., inducing toxicity [3] or reducing toxicity [4]). Therefore, understanding how nanomaterials interact with protein has become indispensable, driven by the safety design of nanomaterials for potential biological and medical applications [5,6].

Despite the rapidly growing literature on the bio-applications of nanomaterials, the fundamental interactions between nanomaterials and proteins are still not well understood [7]. A large number of studies have been carried out to obtain the related information by using circular dichroism (CD), isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), UV/fluorescence spectroscopy and other techniques [8]. These methods facilitate the exploration of molecular details including the macroscopic composition, the secondary structure and conformation of the protein [9]. For example, Luo et al. [10] applied CD to monitor the changes of secondary structure of Aβ peptide, and Cedervall et al. [11] showed that ITC could be used to study the affinity and stoichiometry of protein-nanoparticles interactions. However, to the best of our knowledge, the information of the interaction mechanism has been limited because the characterization of the interaction processes at the atom or even residue level remains challenging.

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) has been applied to monitor protein-target (protein, drug or dye molecular) interactions using two-dimensional HSQC experiments with 15N-labeled proteins, which provides high-resolution information of the protein structure and changes by the analysis of the NMR chemical shift perturbation (CSP) [12,13]. CSP is an efficient technique to identity the interaction sites by detecting the chemical environment changes of all coupled H and N atoms of the protein upon formation of the protein complexes [14,15]. Therefore, the CSP approach is a powerful method for investigating the interaction of nanomaterials with protein.

Ubiquitin is a small signaling protein with 76 amino acids highly conserved throughout eukaryotes [16], which is ubiquitously involved in all aspects of cellular activities, such as proteasomal degradation [17], immune response [18], and DNA damage repair [19]. It has also been a target protein for studying the interaction involving nanomaterials because of its small size and having one of the lowest molecular weight among the functional proteins. As for graphene quantum dots (GQDs), they have attracted intensive interest due to their extraordinary properties [20], and great potentials for the development of bio-applications in bioimaging, drug carrier, and so on [21]. Therefore, in this study we chose ubiquitin-GQDs as the representative model to investigate the protein-nanomaterials interaction.

Graphene quantum dots (diameter of ∼5 nm) were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd, and used in the experiments without any further purification. Ubiquitin was expressed in the E. coli BL21-star cells. The proteins were purified by chromatography through the sequential application of Sepharose-S, Superdex-75 and Source-Q columns.

The size and distribution of GQDs were investigated by the high resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, TecnaiG2, FEI) and the atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Icon, Bruker). Further, the UV-Vis spectroscopy (UV-3600, Shimadzu) and steady state fluorescence spectroscopy (FLS980, Edinburgh) were used for the identification of GQDs based on their optical properties.

Samples were prepared by mixing the protein solutions with GQDs solutions at a GQDs/ubiquitin mass ratio of 0:1 (i.e., free ubiquitin), 1:1, 1:1, 1:7 and 4:1 (GQDs free solutions were used as the negative controls). All of the samples were shaken at 310 K for 4 h. CD spectra were recorded on a JASCO J-810, Japan instrument using quartz cells with a path length of 10 mm. The resulting spectra were corrected by subtracting the background signal of GQDs.

OtetRED96 (ForteBio) equipment was used to quantitatively characterize the binding interactions between GQDs and the protein. The total working volume for samples or buffer was 200 μL per well with an agitated speed of 1000 rpm. Aminopropysilane (APS) biosensor tips were prewetted for at least 5 min in distilled water immediately prior to the assay. Afterward, The GQDs were noncovalently loaded to saturation onto the APS tips. The negative controls were prepared using the same loading process without the addition of the nanomaterials solutions. GQDs loaded tips were washed in PBS for balance and transferred to the wells containing different concentrations of ubiquitin. Associations were measured for about 120 s and dissociations were monitored until equilibrium was reached. The kinetic parameters (Kon and Koff) and dissociation constants (KD) were calculated by using the Octet software.

Samples for NMR experiments were prepared by dissolving 15N-labeled ubiquitin in a 10 mM PBS buffer with 10% D2O to a final concentration of 0.5 mM, while the concentration of GQDs at a mass ratio of 4:1. 1H 15N HSQC experiments were collected at 298 K on a Bruker 700 MHz spectrometer equipped with a cryogenic probe. Data were processed with the same processing parameters for all samples using the NMR pipe. Data analysis and peak assignment was performed with the program CCPN analysis V2.4. Chemical shift values derived from the NMR experiments have been analyzed using the QtiPlot software.

All atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were performed on the IBM BlueGene Supercomputer, using NAMD2.9 [22]. We built the ubiquitin model based on its crystal structure (PDB ID: 1UBQ). Because of the coexistence of both sp
2 (graphene-like) and oxidized domains in a typical graphene oxide (GO) nanosheet or GQD, as well as the stronger van der Waals and hydrophobic interaction between graphene (GR) and a protein, we modeled the adsorption of ubiquitin directly on a GR nanosheet according to our previous study [23], where we did simulate a GO model (C10O1(OH)1(COOH)0.5) and found that the differences were small between GO and GR, with GR showing slightly stronger binding strength to membrane lipids. The simulated hexagonal GR sheet is effectively infinite because of the periodic boundaries (Fig. S1 in Supporting Material). The ubiquitin protein was initially placed about 10 Å (the minimal distance between atoms in ubiquitin and the GR surface) above the GR sheet. After that, the complex was solvated in a hexagonal water box that contains 18,974H2O molecules.

Following protocols similar to those used in previous simulations [24–30], each simulation system was first equilibrated at 1 bar and 300 K for 5 ns. During the equilibration, the backbone atoms in the ubiquitin protein and atoms in the GR sheet were constrained. The subsequent simulation was carried out in the NVT (constant particle number N, volume V and temperature T) ensemble without constraining any protein atom. Ten independent simulations with different/random initial orientations of the ubiquitin protein were carried out to sample different ubiquitin-graphene contacts. The Langevin dynamics were applied to oxygen atoms in water to maintain a constant temperature (300 K) in each simulated system. We used the TIP3P model for water [31,32], the standard ionic force field for ions [33], the GR force field for the nanosheets [23], and the CHARMM force field [34] for the ubiquitin protein. A smooth cutoff (10–12 Å) was used to calculate the van der Waals interactions. Electrostatic interactions were calculated using the particle-mesh Ewald (PME) method with a grid size of about 1 Å. The integration time-step in each simulation was set at 1 fs.

The typical transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) images of GQDs are shown in Fig. 1 a–b. The TEM images show that GQDs were of ∼5 nm in diameter and the AFM images show an average thickness at around 0.5–2 nm, which is in accordance with the monolayered graphene [35]. Due to quantum confinement effect, GQDs could exhibit unique optical property [36]. Therefore, after scanning the ultraviolet–visible (UV-Vis) and fluorescence emission spectra, we find that GQDs exhibited an absorption bands of around 280 nm in the UV-Vis spectrum (Fig. 1c) and the strongest emission of the GQDs occurs at around 450 nm (Fig. 1d), which are correspondent to the structure of nanodots. The above data confirmed the identity of the GQDs.

Due to the strong hydrophobic interaction, it is easy for ubiquitin to bind to the surface of GQDs forming adducts. In order to understand the interactions between GQDs and ubiquitin, binding affinities were measured using an Octet RED96 Surface Plasmon Resonance system. Briefly, GQDs were adsorbed onto the streptavidin (SA) capture sensors until saturation. Subsequently, the GQDs-coated SA sensors were incubated with ubiquitin over a concentration gradient to measure the corresponding association and dissociation signals (Fig. 2 a). The binding constant values are estimated as 1.90 E-08 M (∼10.5 kcal/mol), which indicates a high binding affinity of ubiquitin to GQDs.

To explore whether the interaction of ubiquitin with GQDs causes a conformational change in the protein, we further performed the circular dichroism (CD) measurements. As shown in Fig. 2b, the presence of GQDs induced a marked change at around 215 nm in the CD spectrum of ubiquitin, which is considered typical for the reduction of β-sheet component of protein [37], demonstrating that the observed adsorption processes were accompanied by substantial changes in the protein secondary structure.

A series of two-dimensional 1H 15N NMR experiments were conducted to monitor the chemical shift perturbations (CSPs) of 15N isotopically labeled ubiquitin upon addition of GQDs. The CSPs were calculated using the following equation, where ΔδH and ΔδN are the shift differences of the amide hydrogen and nitrogen respectively.

This equation [38] is chosen to combine the changes in δ N and δ H, but reduce the weighting on the 15N chemical shift change, which covers ∼100–140 ppm in contrast to 6–10 ppm for the 1H dimension. Fig. 3 a shows the superposition of 1H 15N HSQC spectra of the ubiquitin and GQDs-ubiquitin complex. The NMR spectra between ubiquitin and the corona revealed that only a few resonances of amide groups experienced significant differences in CSPs upon interacting with GQDs. The CSP for each amide NH group of the protein backbone is presented in Fig. 3b. The amide peaks of ubiquitin at pH of 7.2 in 1H 15N HSQC experiment have been previously assigned [39]. The large majority of NH groups have CSP smaller than 0.01 ppm in the presence of GQDs, while there are a subset of peaks of ubiquitin shift progressively with CSPs over 0.01 ppm, including Q2, T14, E16, D32, K33, I36, L43, V70 and L71. Most of these residues are located near the β-sheet regions of ubiquitin, consistent with the results of our CD experiments that the interaction was accompanied by substantial reduction in the β-sheet.

Before an adsorption event of ubiquitin on graphene occurred, the ubiquitin initially underwent a random diffusion away from the graphene and rotated freely in water. When the ubiquitin diffusively approached the graphene, the anchoring of ubiquitin on the graphene was dependent on the contacting residues. For example, as shown in the movie (UBQ-GPH.mpg in the supporting material), two trials of ubiquitin's adsorption onto the graphene nanosheet failed because the contact was initiated by the non-hydrophobic residues (such as K63). In contrast, the following successful adsorption (as shown in the same movie) was initiated by the hydrophobic residue P37. Thus, the hydrophobic interaction between ubiquitin and graphene is critical in initiating the adsorption. Following the initial contact, more nearby residues including the hydrophilic ones were in contact with the graphene, accompanied with a slight rotation of ubiquitin on the graphene surface. Consequently, the ubiquitin can only diffuse along the 2D graphene surface and cannot rotate any more. Therefore, the interface between ubiquitin and graphene is well defined after the adsorption, and residues near the interface are shown in Fig. 4 .

In all ten independent simulations, the adsorption of ubiquitin on the graphene was found unanimously, and the secondary structure of ubiquitin was intact after the adsorption (see Fig. S2 in Supporting Material). However, the found interfaces between ubiquitin and graphene could be quite different. We categorize all ten interfaces into two types: with (Type 1) and without (Type 2) the flexible C-terminal of ubiquitin (residue numbers from 70 to 76). From the analysis of the simulation trajectories, we obtained the contact probability of each residue in ubiquitin with the graphene. Numerically, a residue is considered to be in contact with the graphene if any of its heavy atoms is within 5 Å of the graphene. If the distance of the closest heavy atom of a residue to the graphene is between 5 and 6 Å, it is regarded as in partial contact, otherwise it is treated as no contact. The calculated contact-probabilities of all residues in ubiquitin for the ten different simulations are shown in Fig. 4a–j, among which Fig. 4 (a, c, d, f, g, h, j) show the Type 1 contacts (with the C-terminal) and Fig. 4 (b, e, i) show the Type 2 contacts (without the C-terminal). The direct interaction energy between ubiquitin and graphene was calculated from the contacts in each simulation (Fig. 4k). Systematically, the interaction energies for the Type 1 contacts are lower (less favorable) than those for the Type 2 contacts.

Experimentally, residues V70 and L71 in the C-terminal of ubiquitin were found to have very high CSP values, which is reflected in their corresponding high contact probabilities found in the majority of the Type 2 (also more favorable) contacts as shown in Fig. 4a, c, d, g, j. Although the CSP value of L43 is at the intermediate level, there is no observation of any contact between L43 and the graphene during the simulation due to its side chain pointing towards the hydrophobic core of ubiquitin. Nevertheless, its two neighboring residues, R42 and I44, were found inside the contact (Type 2) as shown in Fig. 4c, d, f, g, h, j. A snapshot of the contacts including residues V70, L71, R42 and I44 is illustrated in Fig. 4n. The residue V36 was indicated to have a non-negligible CSP in experiment which was also observed to appear at the interface from one of the simulations with the Type 2 contact (see Fig. 4a and l).

For the Type 1 contact, residues Q2, T14, D16 and K33 with high CSP values were also found in one simulation (Fig. 4b). A snapshot of the contacts with these four residues is illustrated in Fig. 4m. In the other two simulations with the Type 1 contact (Fig. 4e–i), the residues with higher contact probabilities (such as S20 and N60) do not have high experimental CSP values. However, the corresponding interaction energies between ubiquitin and graphene in these two systems are also very high (see simulation #2 and #6 in Fig. 4k), leading to an unstable complex. Thus, the contacts found in these two simulations might be at an intermediate state with an averaged lifetime of 100 ns, which could not be captured using NMR.

Overall, our simulation results highlighted multiple different interfacial contacts between ubiquitin and graphene. For residues experimentally identified with non-negligible CSP values, their corresponding probabilities of contact with the graphene were also found high in the simulations. Thus, our simulation results reasonably agree with the experimental ones. It should be noted that there is no significant β-sheet content reduction observed in our simulation as seen in the CD spectroscopy, probably due to the limited simulation time [40]. By identifying the interaction sites from the NMR experiment and molecular simulation, we found the majority of these amino acids, such as P37, V70 and L71, were hydrophobic, and therefore the origin of such interaction is largely hydrophobic interaction, in addition to van der Waals forces.

In summary, we studied the interaction between GQDs and ubiquitin by employing both experimental methods and molecular dynamics simulations, and demonstrated the high binding affinity between ubiquitin and GQDs. The analysis of the protein secondary structure showed that the interaction induced a marked reduction in the β-sheet of ubiquitin. Further, we identified the ubiquitin-GQDs interaction sites at the residue level using nuclear magnetic resonance technology, and found that the binding interfaces were mostly on the β-sheet regions of ubiquitin, which was further confirmed by our molecular simulations. Our work provided a fundamental understanding of the interaction mechanism of nanoparticles with protein through identification and characterization of the key “binding sites”, with a combined experimental and theoretical approach. Our work can be readily extended to study other protein-nanoparticle complex, providing guidance on the safe design and development of potential applications of nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is partially supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program Grant No. 2014CB931900), National Natural Science Foundation of China (11575123, 11574224 and 11374221), Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Radiation Medicine and Protection, a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article: the set-ups of MD simulation system, the time-dependent secondary structure of ubiquitin during the adsorption on graphene and the movie UBQ-GPH.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.05.096.
